Myofibrils are rod-like structures that run the entire length of the muscle fibre, as shown in Figure 2.3a. The number of myofibrils present within a muscle cell can vary but there are thought to be around 2000 in an adult muscle fibre. Myofibrils can be considered as the *contractile apparatus* of the muscle fibre as they contain the contractile proteins actin and myosin. Collectively, actin and myosin comprise 85% of total muscle protein, with myosin taking the lion's share (60%). The actin protein is 42 kDa in size and is often referred to as the *thin filament*, whereas myosin is referred to as the *thick filament* because it is 480 kDa.

The structure of the thick and thin filaments is shown in Figure 2.4. Note the unique shape of myosin molecules in that each molecule is shaped like two golf clubs twisted together – the myosin tail (i.e. the golf club shafts) points towards the centre of the sarcomere (the *M-line*). The myosin heads (often referred to as the crossbridges) project outward from the shaft in a spiralling fashion and extend towards one of the six thin filaments that surround each thick filament.

Actin and myosin are known as *myofibrillar* proteins because they are located in the myofibrils of muscle cells, and are sometimes referred to as the *motor proteins* as they are responsible for muscle contraction. Essentially, these proteins turn the chemical energy stored within the bonds of ATP into mechanical work, and therefore form the molecular basis for muscle contraction.

## Definition

Myofibrils are rod-like contractile structures running the entire length of the muscle fibre, containing the myofibrillar proteins actin and myosin which form the molecular basis for muscle contraction. An adult muscle fibre is thought to contain around 2000 myofibrils.

## Core Principles

- Myofibrils run the entire length of the muscle fibre, with approximately 2000 myofibrils present in an adult muscle fibre.
- Myofibrils house the contractile apparatus of the muscle fibre, comprising actin (thin filament, 42 kDa) and myosin (thick filament, 480 kDa).
- Actin and myosin together account for 85% of total muscle protein (myosin ~60%).
- Actin and myosin are termed *myofibrillar proteins* because they are located in the myofibrils of muscle cells, and are also referred to as *motor proteins* because they are responsible for muscle contraction.
- These motor proteins convert the chemical energy stored within the bonds of ATP into mechanical work, forming the molecular basis for muscle contraction.
- Each myosin molecule is shaped like two golf clubs twisted together; the tail points towards the M-line at the centre of the sarcomere, while the heads (crossbridges) project outward in a spiralling fashion towards one of the six surrounding thin filaments.

## Sub-concepts

- **Thin filament (actin):** 42 kDa protein component of the myofibril.
- **Thick filament (myosin):** 480 kDa protein; tails converge at the M-line, heads form crossbridges with actin.
- **Crossbridges:** the projecting myosin heads that interact with the surrounding six thin filaments.
- **Myofibrillar proteins / motor proteins:** functional designation of actin and myosin reflecting their role in transducing ATP-derived chemical energy into mechanical work.
- **Myofibril abundance:** approximately 2000 myofibrils per adult muscle fibre, though the number can vary between cells.

## Practical Applications

- Understanding myofibrillar protein structure and function underpins the study of muscle contraction, ATP utilisation during exercise, and adaptations of contractile machinery to training.
